Tennis Automated | ATP Fantasy | Week 3 Pre-Draw

Week 3 Cheat Sheet

Pre-draw edition for 20-26 April 2026. Week 3 moves to a standalone Madrid ATP Masters 1000 slate, and the board is already tightening: Jannik Sinner arrives off the Monte-Carlo title and back at World No. 1, Carlos Alcaraz is already stacking wins in Barcelona, and the edge comes from balancing elite title equity against Week 2 workload before the Madrid bracket lands.

Week Window 20-26 April
Slate Madrid only
Surface Clay altitude
Key Date Event starts: 22 Apr

Tournament Lens

Top Of Board

Madrid's official entry list says the field will feature all current Top 10 players in the PIF ATP Rankings. Jannik Sinner arrives as the reigning Monte-Carlo champion and current World No. 1, Carlos Alcaraz owns a 15-2 Madrid record with two titles, Novak Djokovic is a three-time champion, and Casper Ruud returns as defending champion. Former winners Alexander Zverev and Andrey Rublev stay relevant because Madrid rewards serve-plus-first-ball patterns more than slower clay stops.

  • Best home-soil ceiling: Alcaraz
  • Best recent Masters form: Sinner
  • Best Madrid-specific serve case: Zverev
  • Best veteran leverage: Djokovic

Fantasy Shape

Madrid is not a normal clay week. The altitude keeps first-strike tennis live, which means serve bonus hunters matter more here than they do in slower clay events. The event runs from 22 April to 3 May, including a stadium exhibition at the Santiago Bernabeu, so pre-draw fantasy builds should start with players who have real semi-final equity and then layer in upside values such as Joao Fonseca, Ben Shelton, and Andrey Rublev once the bracket shows where the traffic lands.

  • Prioritize semi-final equity over one-match upside.
  • Give extra weight to ace production in Madrid conditions.
  • Wait for the draw before stacking one half too aggressively.
  • Watch Barcelona and Munich finalists for turnaround fatigue and hidden physical flags.

What Matters This Week

ATP Fantasy still gives you a 100-credit budget, six starters, two alternates, and one Bonus Ball player who scores double. The scoring rules do not change, but a standalone ATP Masters 1000 week changes the math because deep advancement is worth more than simply surviving into Thursday.

Player prices are still based on the PIF ATP Live Rankings, which means the pre-draw edge comes from identifying ranking-driven mispricings before the market settles. Madrid especially rewards players who can hold serve cheaply and finish points early, but that upside only matters if the route is not crowded and the Week 2 workload is manageable.

+2 per ace +10 straight-sets win +20 upset win -2 per double fault -20 upset loss
Best Week 3 Angles
  • Start with one of Sinner or Alcaraz as your title anchor.
  • Treat Madrid as faster clay, not generic clay.
  • Use live-ranking risers such as Fonseca and Vacherot as pricing checks.
  • Be ready to fade famous names if the draw packs their quarter or Week 2 load gets heavy.

Last Week's Top Performers

Best scorer: Jannik Sinner

Sinner beat Carlos Alcaraz in the Monte-Carlo final to claim his first clay-court Masters 1000 title. ATP also noted that he matched Novak Djokovic's 2015 feat by winning the first three Masters 1000 events of a season, extended his Masters 1000 winning streak to 22 matches, and began his 67th week at No. 1 on Monday.

Best elite floor: Carlos Alcaraz

Alcaraz did not lift the Monte-Carlo trophy, but he still reached the final and immediately bounced back in Barcelona, beating Otto Virtanen while keeping his Madrid case intact. ATP also noted that he owns a 15-2 record in Madrid, which is why he stays near the top of the Week 3 board even after the Monte-Carlo loss.

Best outperformer: Valentin Vacherot

Vacherot was the clearest one-week overperformer. ATP reported that he beat Lorenzo Musetti and Alex de Minaur on his way to the Monte-Carlo semi-finals, became the first Monegasque player to reach that stage in the Principality, and surged to No. 17 in the live rankings during the run.

Best under-the-radar carryover: Joao Fonseca

Fonseca is carrying the cleanest momentum into Week 3 outside the elite tier. ATP highlighted that he reached his first ATP Masters 1000 quarter-final in Monte-Carlo, then beat Alejandro Tabilo in cold Munich conditions to climb to No. 31 in the live rankings, which is exactly the kind of ranking-versus-upside gap fantasy managers should track.

Bonus Ball Board

1. Carlos Alcaraz

Until the draw says otherwise, Alcaraz remains the default Week 3 Bonus Ball. He combines the best Madrid-specific record with home-soil comfort and the strongest pre-draw event history on the board.

2. Jannik Sinner

Sinner's Masters floor is currently unmatched, and he now arrives with the Monte-Carlo title and the No. 1 ranking back in hand. If you want the cleanest pre-draw floor instead of the home-soil ceiling, he is the alternative Bonus Ball.

3. Alexander Zverev

Zverev has the kind of serve-plus-first-ball profile that plays well in Madrid altitude, and he already owns a title here. His gritty Munich opener also matters because it kept the form line alive after another Monte-Carlo semi-final.

Core Plays

Carlos Alcaraz

The best combination of event history, ceiling and likely ownership. If his quarter is not overloaded, he stays central to Week 3 builds.

Jannik Sinner

Even on clay, Sinner is still giving fantasy managers the cleanest elite floor because he wins service games efficiently and rarely leaks points in ugly patches.

Alexander Zverev

Zverev remains a strong pre-draw core play because the Madrid conditions help his first serve, his event history is real, and his current clay form is sturdy enough that he does not need a perfect bracket to matter.

Value Targets

Joao Fonseca

Fonseca remains the cleanest upside value archetype in ATP Fantasy. His ranking still trails his actual threat level on big stages, and Madrid's conditions can amplify his serve-plus-forehand damage.

Ben Shelton

Madrid is one of the few clay stops where Shelton's ace profile can carry genuine weekly value. If his draw is not packed with elite returners, the scoring format can do the rest.

Valentin Vacherot

If ATP Fantasy prices Vacherot off raw rank instead of current level, he becomes one of the more interesting upside checks on the board. Monte-Carlo showed he can handle big-stage clay against top names.

Andrey Rublev

Rublev is not a bargain-bin value, but he is the kind of former champion who can still come in cheaper than his Madrid-specific upside. That makes him one of the more important pre-draw pricing checks.

Trap List

Casper Ruud at full defending-champion tax

The Madrid title run is real, but fantasy is about this week's path and price, not last year's points. If Ruud is priced aggressively and his draw is crowded, he can become an easy overpay.

Novak Djokovic without draw clarity

Djokovic always carries ceiling, but the pre-draw version of Madrid is exactly where brand-name gravity can outrun actual lineup value. Quarter placement matters too much to lock him blindly.

Anyone arriving heavy from Barcelona or Munich

Back-to-back clay weeks can flip from momentum to fatigue quickly. If a player goes deep in Week 2, picks up a physical issue, or lands a rough Madrid opener, the risk profile changes immediately.

Pre-draw edition updated on 15 April 2026 using official ATP Madrid field information, official Monte-Carlo results, and current Barcelona and Munich form signals.
